title: How endotoxins affect your gut health

chunks.title: Introduction to Resistant Starch
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the mainstream narrative around resistant starch and its purported benefits for the microbiome. They express initial enthusiasm for incorporating resistant starch into their diet, citing various types of sources such as potato starch.
chunks.transcript: mainstream nutritional ideas or that resistant starch is second only to um you know an apparition from the Virgin Mary or something it's like it's so holy and wonderful and I remember when I first learned about resistant starch The Narrative was all it's great for your microbiome eat as much of it as you can I ordered some like potato starch on Amazon and I was just throwing like resistant potato starch in my food and this is this is a totally different um a paradigm shift to say that that

chunks.title: Microbiome and Gram-Negative Bacteria
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts towards the microbiome's role in health, particularly the balance between beneficial and harmful bacteria. The speaker highlights the potential dangers of increased populations of gram-negative bacteria that may lead to endotoxin problems when they translocate into the bloodstream.
chunks.transcript: starches or or even do you think soluble fiber could also do this because it's going to feed the microbiome and it's going to increase gram negatives and then we can get into some discussion of soluble versus insoluble fiber but soluble fiber found in things like beans or grains lentils um this I I this is an interesting piece of like sort of the repeat philosophy that you've done so much good work to discuss with people and promulgate is this idea that like this is I think it's so fascinating that that increasing the microbiome in the colon the like the full number of bacteria and the populations may not always be a good thing for people because then you're getting more gram-negative bacteria

chunks.title: Effects of Soluble Fiber and Pectin
chunks.summary: Pectin is introduced as a common component in processed foods that may be linked to gastrointestinal cancers. The speaker elaborates on how soluble fiber could contribute to issues related to endotoxin absorption and gastrointestinal health.
chunks.transcript: which are going to turn over you're gonna get this lipopolysaccharide part of the cell wall also known as endotoxin and we know I don't think anyone would argue that when endotoxin moves across the colonic lining into the bloodstream or even the small intestinal lining into the bloodstream we have massive problems yeah and the soluble fibers are specific I think there's very good research showed that pectin which is in virtually all processed foods out there especially the yogurts and any of the Milkshakes that you're drinking almost all of them have even modified cornstarch which I love I mean there's no there's no need to discuss because it's also GMO and this is it's got its own problems now showing to be allergenic because of the genes that have been modified but pectin itself is now being been shown to be strongly associated with all types of GI cancers the of the large intestine of the small intestine and even gastric cancer and also with liver cancer which kind of demonstrates that pectin probably increases not only the endotoxin but also the its absorption into the bloodstream because endotoxin is now known to be a known carcinogen for hepatocellular carcinoma so the all

chunks.title: Chronic Inflammation and Digestive Issues
chunks.summary: The segment explores conditions like small intestine bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) and the impact of chronic inflammation on gastrointestinal health. The use of antibiotics to manage bacteria in the gut is discussed, alongside the benefits observed in patients undergoing bowel surgery.
chunks.transcript: of these everything that can give this bacteria to feed on specifically the carbohydrates whether they're resistant or not it's going to create a problem now the reason uh we're saying that eat more easily digestible carbs not it's not because they cannot feed this bacteria it's because they get absorbed before they actually get rid that reach the cob and in people with low metabolism or chronic inflammation some of that bacteria starts to creep up into the small intestine and now there's unofficially recognized condition sibo small intestine bacterial overgrowth and and people are given this antibiotic that is called rifaximin which is non-absorbed right yeah it travels through the through all the all the three portions of the track it has a remarkable effect not only on the sidewalk but these people feel generally better the inflammatory markers go down uh their total cholesterol the LDL total and LDL cholesterol go down CRP goes down so it looks like just just this presence of the bacteria and all of its and byproducts are not good for us right so it's good to keep this bacteria probably in check several studies notice that people that are preparing for bowel surgery they often get a very high doses of antibiotics to kind of completely sterilize the intestine I think it's like a cocktail of neomycin and Gentamicin some kind of a strong cocktail combination so they get it for several days before the bowel surgery and then after that doctors notice that these people do remarkably well and actually lose weight depending on how how sterilized their gut is to the point where these people actually started

chunks.title: Dietary Choices and Microbial Balance
chunks.summary: The speaker emphasizes making dietary choices that minimize harm to the intestinal lining and bacterial growth. They advocate for simpler carbohydrates over resistant starches, suggesting that natural sources like fruits can be beneficial without the negative effects of excessive fiber.

chunks.title: Biomarkers of Endotoxin Exposure
chunks.summary: The discussion turns towards potential biomarkers for assessing endotoxin exposure, such as LPS binding protein. The link between chronic diseases and endotoxin levels in the bloodstream is mentioned, highlighting a need for further research in this area.
chunks.transcript: um that the soluble fiber can feed the bacteria in the colon and potentially cause imbalances I don't think we're suggesting you want to sterilize your gut like I said is it practical yeah impractical for humans but it is interesting that some of the foods that we eat that have traditionally canonically been told to us as healthy like lentils and beans and Grains could be contributing to this problem and things like starches cornstarch resistant starch cooked in cooled potatoes I mean that's a favorite one for people for resistant starch you probably don't want to do that or you want to have some Metric on the back end to make sure that your endotoxin isn't going up so one of the more interesting things I came across was this LPS binding protein have you ever seen anyone measure that with a lab the next time I get my blood work done I want to get LBP levels you know LPS binding protein I mean usual doctors can probably order it I have not seen a way to order directly from one of these direct to customer Labs like digital listed I mean they may be able because they have a doctoral staff too they just don't advertise it and I called several of them that said no we I mean we know about it but it's not on the list and we don't want to do something that's like off the list um but uh it's I think it's a very good biomarker another one that you mentioned the last one is soluble City 14 uh another biomarker of chronic systemic endotoxin exposure interestingly it's a very good predictor
